scenario: A city has a limited transport budget and must choose one major intervention for the year.
option_A: Build a central bus corridor that reduces travel time for the largest number of commuters.
option_B: Reduce fares on routes used mainly by low-income neighborhoods.
option_C: Adopt a uniform safety rule that applies to all transport operators and passengers without exception.

scenario: A public hospital has funding for only one improvement program.
option_A: Expand the outpatient unit that serves the highest number of patients per day.
option_B: Create a special treatment fund for patients who cannot otherwise afford basic care.
option_C: Implement a hospital rule requiring equal informed consent procedures for every patient.

scenario: A university wants to improve student support but has a limited annual budget.
option_A: Develop an online advising system that can serve the largest share of students.
option_B: Provide targeted academic support for students from under-resourced schools.
option_C: Adopt a consistent appeals procedure that treats every student case according to the same rule.

scenario: A government is designing a food support program during a drought.
option_A: Distribute food in a way that reaches the highest number of households overall.
option_B: Prioritize households with children, elderly members, or no stable income.
option_C: Create a transparent rule for eligibility and apply it consistently to every applicant.

scenario: A city is choosing a digital public-service platform.
option_A: Build the platform that helps the largest number of citizens complete services faster.
option_B: Add offline access points for communities with poor internet access.
option_C: Require that the system protect every citizen's personal data under the same standard.

scenario: A school district is choosing how to allocate new learning materials.
option_A: Send the materials to schools where they will improve average exam scores the most.
option_B: Send the materials first to schools with the fewest existing resources.
option_C: Use a fixed allocation rule that every school can understand and challenge if needed.

scenario: A ministry is deciding how to reduce unemployment.
option_A: Fund a broad job-matching system expected to place the largest total number of workers.
option_B: Create training programs for young people from regions with the highest unemployment.
option_C: Adopt hiring standards that prevent using people merely as cheap temporary labor.

scenario: A city wants to reduce air pollution but has limited enforcement capacity.
option_A: Target the largest pollution sources to reduce total emissions as much as possible.
option_B: Protect neighborhoods that have suffered the highest pollution burden for years.
option_C: Apply a consistent emissions rule to all firms regardless of political influence.

scenario: A public agency is allocating cybersecurity support to local institutions.
option_A: Protect the systems whose failure would affect the largest number of users.
option_B: Support small clinics and schools that lack technical staff and are most exposed.
option_C: Set a minimum security standard that every institution must meet without exceptions.

scenario: A region is distributing clean-water infrastructure.
option_A: Build the system that gives safe water access to the largest number of residents.
option_B: Begin with remote villages that have been excluded from previous infrastructure projects.
option_C: Use a universal access rule that recognizes safe water as something owed to every resident.

scenario: A country is planning vaccination outreach.
option_A: Focus on locations where outreach will produce the largest increase in total vaccination coverage.
option_B: Prioritize communities with poor health access and higher vulnerability.
option_C: Use a rule that every person must be offered clear information and voluntary consent.

scenario: A university is developing an AI tool for admission support.
option_A: Use the tool if it improves processing speed for the largest number of applications.
option_B: Adjust the system to reduce disadvantages faced by applicants from low-resource schools.
option_C: Require a rule that no applicant is judged by a system they cannot appeal.

scenario: A city is deciding where to place new street lights.
option_A: Install lights in the busiest areas to reduce the highest number of nighttime incidents.
option_B: Install lights first in neglected neighborhoods with the highest safety concerns.
option_C: Apply a transparent placement rule based on public criteria, not political pressure.

scenario: A public broadcaster is choosing educational programming.
option_A: Broadcast lessons that benefit the largest number of students preparing for national exams.
option_B: Create programs for students in rural areas with limited teacher access.
option_C: Ensure every program respects students as learners rather than treating them only as test-score units.

scenario: A government is designing a cash-transfer program.
option_A: Select the design expected to produce the greatest total reduction in poverty indicators.
option_B: Prioritize households with the least income and fewest support options.
option_C: Apply an eligibility rule that can be publicly justified and consistently followed.

scenario: A municipality is deciding how to respond to informal street vending.
option_A: Adopt the approach that improves traffic flow and public order for the largest number of residents.
option_B: Provide protected vending zones for people who depend on vending for survival.
option_C: Use a rule that respects each vendor's dignity and avoids arbitrary removal.

scenario: A hospital must decide how to schedule non-emergency surgeries after a backlog.
option_A: Schedule cases to reduce the total waiting time across all patients.
option_B: Prioritize patients whose health and income were most harmed by the delay.
option_C: Follow a public scheduling rule that treats comparable cases in the same way.

scenario: A ministry is introducing digital identity cards.
option_A: Deploy first in urban areas where the largest number of people can be registered quickly.
option_B: Start with groups who struggle most to access services without identification.
option_C: Require that every registration process respects consent, privacy, and appeal rights.

scenario: A school is deciding how to use a limited scholarship fund.
option_A: Award scholarships to students whose success is expected to produce the largest community benefit.
option_B: Award scholarships to capable students from the poorest households.
option_C: Use a rule that all applicants are judged by the same transparent criteria.

scenario: A city is selecting a climate adaptation project.
option_A: Fund the project that protects the largest population from flood damage.
option_B: Fund protections for settlements that face the highest risk and have the fewest resources.
option_C: Adopt a planning rule that does not expose some residents to danger for others' convenience.

scenario: A public library system is choosing a technology upgrade.
option_A: Digitize the most borrowed materials to benefit the largest number of users.
option_B: Provide devices and access support for users who cannot afford them.
option_C: Create access rules that treat all users as entitled to respectful service.

scenario: A police department is revising community safety policy.
option_A: Use resources where they reduce the largest number of reported incidents.
option_B: Invest in communities that have experienced both high crime and historic neglect.
option_C: Adopt rules that prevent treating any person merely as a tool for statistics.

scenario: A government is allocating agricultural support.
option_A: Fund crops and regions that will increase total food production the most.
option_B: Support smallholder farmers who have the least access to credit and equipment.
option_C: Apply a procurement rule that all farmers can understand and contest.

scenario: A city is designing emergency evacuation plans.
option_A: Choose routes that move the largest number of people out of danger fastest.
option_B: Prioritize assistance for people with disabilities, elderly residents, and isolated communities.
option_C: Use a rule that protects every person's right to safe evacuation procedures.

scenario: A university is deciding whether to use remote proctoring.
option_A: Use remote proctoring if it allows the largest number of students to take exams efficiently.
option_B: Provide alternatives for students with weak internet, shared rooms, or limited devices.
option_C: Reject any system that monitors students in ways that violate dignity and informed consent.

scenario: A health agency is choosing a malaria-prevention strategy.
option_A: Distribute nets where modeling predicts the largest reduction in total infections.
option_B: Target communities with the highest vulnerability and poorest clinic access.
option_C: Use a public rule that every household can understand and appeal if excluded.

scenario: A city is allocating housing support.
option_A: Build the housing units that shelter the largest number of people within the budget.
option_B: Prioritize families facing eviction and people with no safe alternative.
option_C: Adopt a rule that prevents arbitrary exclusion from housing assistance.

scenario: A ministry is deciding how to regulate AI in schools.
option_A: Allow AI tools where they improve learning outcomes for the largest number of students.
option_B: Support schools with fewer teachers so AI reduces existing educational gaps.
option_C: Require that AI tools respect student privacy and do not replace human responsibility.

scenario: A public health office is distributing mental-health services.
option_A: Fund the service model that reaches the largest number of clients.
option_B: Prioritize groups with high need and limited access to care.
option_C: Set a rule that every client is treated with dignity and informed choice.

scenario: A city is planning road maintenance.
option_A: Repair the roads used by the highest number of travelers each day.
option_B: Repair roads in neglected areas where poor conditions limit access to work and school.
option_C: Use a transparent maintenance rule that prevents favoritism and arbitrary decisions.
